original_abstract:
While higher education institutions frequently champion the values of equity, diversity, inclusion, and Indigenization (EDI&I) in their mission statements and public discourse, empirical evidence suggests that meaningful structural progress remains stagnant. This paper argues that the gap between institutional rhetoric and substantive change is maintained through "institutional gaslighting"—a process by which universities adopt the language of EDI&I while simultaneously utilizing administrative and social maneuvers to frustrate efforts to dismantle systemic inequality. 

Drawing on two case studies involving large-scale intersectional data collection—a leadership audit in Canada and a study of faculty bias in Australia—the authors illustrate how data-driven attempts to expose systemic barriers are met with profound resistance. The article identifies and conceptualizes four distinct modes of institutional gaslighting: the *slowdown* (using bureaucracy to delay progress), the *pushback* (questioning the methodological validity or professional legitimacy of advocates), the *shutdown* (halting initiatives entirely by citing conflicting norms), and the *blowback* (exerting social and professional pressure to silence researchers). By mapping these strategies of resistance, the paper offers a framework for understanding how formal and informal institutional norms protect the status quo. Ultimately, the authors advocate for a "politics of refusal" to counter these manipulative dynamics and advance genuine structural transformation within the academy.
humanized_abstract:
There is pervasive structural stagnation in EDI&I in post-secondary education even though universities champion these values, seemingly without end, in all manner of institutional documents and communications. What explains this disconnect? This article makes a theoretical argument that such stagnation is maintained by what we term “institutional gaslighting”, a phenomenon where universities adopting EDI&I discourses concurrently rely on administrative and social strategies that act as obstacles to addressing root causes of structural inequality in the institution.

Data that reveals the constraints experienced by members of marginalised groups is not always welcomed in environments where societal arrangements based on inequality and discrimination are the norm. In the journal Gender, Work and Organization, Jamie Cross and David Hart are citing two instances of “data turbulence” where members of minority groups had undertaken an exercise to gather comprehensive and qualitative data about their experiences (a leadership audit in the case of women at the University of Calgary and a study of the bias that academic women encounter in relation to their publication activities at Monash University in Australia). The article explains that in both cases the results were met with enormous pushback and it highlights four manifestations of “institutional gaslighting” identified in the turbulent events surrounding the release of this kind of data: - A slowdown: this is characterised by bureaucrats prolonging the time necessary to deal with the issues raised through the data and by actions taken to downplay their significance. - A pushback: in this respect the validity of the methodologies employed to gather the data is contested or else the advocates for change are portrayed as being in some way professionally incompetent or deviant. - A shutdown: in this respect the dominant institution imposes a taboo preventing further discussion on the data and the arguments for change that it may contain; it does more than hinder or obstruct as it silences the opposition in the discourse. - A blowback: in this case, there is a negative social reaction which exerts pressure the authors of the report and/or the opposition groups they worked with, in order to shut them down. The authors seek to contribute to the politics of refusal; in other words, the strategy that refuses to accept the twists and turns imposed by actors aiming to hinder fundamental change in the current organisations and structures.
